type_safety.description: Type annotations and compile-time guarantees — Elixir's built-in gradual type system (1.19+) or @spec/@type for older codebases
look_for: Public API functions with no type information (neither native type annotations nor @spec)
Pattern match clauses that silently drop to a catch-all without logging or raising
Ecto changeset validations that don't match the schema field types
Functions accepting overly broad types (term(), any()) where a specific type is known
Mixed type strategies in the same codebase: some modules use native types, others use @spec, others have nothing
Structs with enforced keys (@enforce_keys) that don't match what callers actually provide
skip: Private helper functions without type annotations (acceptable in Elixir convention)
Phoenix controller actions (framework-typed)
Test modules
Codebases on older Elixir versions using @spec consistently — that is the correct approach pre-1.19

error_consistency.description: Consistent use of {:ok, _}/{:error, _} tuples, rescue blocks, and error propagation
look_for: Mixed error strategies: some functions return {:ok, _}/{:error, _}, others raise, others return nil
Bare rescue without specifying exception type (rescue _ -> or rescue e ->)
with chains that silently pass through unmatched error tuples in the else clause
Functions using raise for expected control flow instead of {:error, reason}
Inconsistent error tuple shapes: {:error, reason} vs {:error, reason, details} vs {:error, {type, msg}}
try/rescue around pure functions that cannot raise
skip: Bang functions (foo!) that intentionally raise — this is Elixir convention
Boundary modules that convert {:error, _} to exceptions for external callers
ExUnit assertions

abstraction_fitness.description: OTP abstractions, behaviour implementations, and module decomposition
look_for: GenServer used where a simple module with functions would suffice (no state needed)
Agent used for complex state that should be a GenServer (needs handle_call logic)
Supervisor strategies that don't match failure domains (one_for_one vs one_for_all)
Deeply nested with chains (>4 clauses) that should be extracted or use a pipeline module
use macros that inject large amounts of code — consider splitting into focused behaviours
Context modules (Phoenix) that mix unrelated domain concerns
Ecto queries built with raw SQL when Ecto.Query composability would be clearer
skip: GenServer/Supervisor patterns required by OTP design
Phoenix-generated context modules with standard CRUD operations
Behaviour implementations that must conform to callback specs

contract_coherence.description: Behaviour callbacks, @callback specs, and module API consistency
look_for: @callback specs that don't match the implementing function's actual return types
Modules using @behaviour but missing required callback implementations
@doc annotations that describe different parameters or return values than the @spec
Public API functions whose @spec allows success but implementation always raises on certain inputs
Plug modules where init/1 and call/2 contracts are inconsistent
skip: Optional callbacks with default implementations
Overridable functions in use macros

cross_module_architecture.description: OTP application boundaries, supervision tree design, and inter-context coupling
look_for: Phoenix contexts calling directly into other contexts instead of through a shared boundary
Circular aliases between modules in different contexts
Supervision tree children that depend on siblings' internal state
Application.get_env/put_env used for cross-module communication instead of proper message passing
Ecto schemas imported/aliased across context boundaries (schema coupling)
LiveView components reaching into contexts they shouldn't know about
skip: Shared schemas for join tables that naturally span contexts
Cross-context queries in reporting/analytics modules
Phoenix.PubSub for legitimate event broadcasting

initialization_coupling.description: Application boot dependencies, compile-time vs runtime config, and start-order fragility
look_for: Application.get_env at module attribute level (compile-time capture of runtime config)
Module attributes that call external services or read files at compile time
Compile-time dependencies that force recompilation cascades (use vs import vs alias)
config.exs values that should be runtime config (config/runtime.exs)
Processes started outside the supervision tree (spawn without link/monitor)
skip: Module attributes for compile-time constants that genuinely don't change
Application.compile_env for values that are legitimately compile-time

convention_outlier.description: Elixir/Phoenix naming conventions, file organization, and community patterns
look_for: Module names that don't follow Elixir CamelCase convention
Functions not following snake_case convention
File paths that don't match module names (MyApp.Foo in lib/my_app/bar.ex)
Inconsistent use of alias vs full module paths across sibling modules
Mixed approaches to similar problems: some modules use Ecto.Multi, others use manual transactions
Phoenix controllers not following RESTful action naming (index, show, create, update, delete)
skip: Macro-generated module names
Protocol implementations following their own naming conventions
Third-party library naming conventions

test_strategy.description: ExUnit test organization, fixture patterns, and coverage strategy
look_for: Critical GenServer/Supervisor modules with no test coverage
Tests that start real processes without proper cleanup (missing on_exit/start_supervised)
Overuse of mocks (Mox) where simple module injection would suffice
Missing integration tests for Ecto multi-step transactions
Test modules without describe blocks for logical grouping
Async tests that share database state without Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox
skip: Phoenix-generated test helpers and support files
Migration test files
Property-based tests with intentionally broad generators
